title-group.article-title: Variant connective tissue as a risk factor for long COVID: a case-control study of data from a retrospective online survey of adults in the USA and UK

contrib-group.aff: 1Clinical Neuroscience, Brighton and Sussex Medical School, Brighton, UK
2Institute of Psychiatry, Psychology & Neuroscience, King’s College London, London, UK
5Sussex Partnership NHS Foundation Trust, Worthing, UK

fn.p: Supplemental material This content has been supplied by the author(s). It has not been vetted by BMJ Publishing Group Limited (BMJ) and may not have been peer-reviewed. Any opinions or recommendations discussed are solely those of the author(s) and are not endorsed by BMJ. BMJ disclaims all liability and responsibility arising from any reliance placed on the content. Where the content includes any translated material, BMJ does not warrant the accuracy and reliability of the translations (including but not limited to local regulations, clinical guidelines, terminology, drug names and drug dosages), and is not responsible for any error and/or omissions arising from translation and adaptation or otherwise.
Additional supplemental material is published online only. To view, please visit the journal online (https://doi.org/10.1136/bmjph-2025-002949).

permissions.copyright-statement: Copyright © Author(s) (or their employer(s)) 2025. Re-use permitted under CC BY-NC. Published by BMJ Group.

license.license-p: This is an open access article distributed in accordance with the Creative Commons Attribution Non Commercial (CC BY-NC 4.0) license, which permits others to distribute, remix, adapt, build upon this work non-commercially, and license their derivative works on different terms, provided the original work is properly cited, appropriate credit is given, any changes made indicated, and the use is non-commercial. See: http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0/.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: This study explored the extent to which two measures of joint hypermobility, a marker of variant connective tissue, predict the development of long COVID after COVID-19 infection, and whether the severity of initial COVID-19 symptoms impacts this relationship.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: We recruited 1816 participants (352 (19.4%) reporting long COVID﻿Long COVID, 1464 (80,6%) not reporting long COVIDLong COVID) from the US and UK for a retrospective online survey. The primary outcome was self-reported long COVIDLong COVID, defined as experiencing symptoms related to a COVID-19 infection at least 3 months after infection. Secondary outcomes included severity of symptoms during each COVID-19 infection, generalised joint hypermobility (GJH), and the novel concept of 'extreme hypermobility'.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: In separate binomial logistic regressions controlling for sex assigned at birth, age, number of infections and number of vaccine doses, both GJH (OR 1.29, 95% CI 1.00 to 1.65) and extreme hypermobility (OR 2.12, 95% CI 1.43 to 3.16) were found to be predictive of long COVID﻿﻿﻿﻿﻿﻿Long COVID. This likely occurs through two pathways. First, both GJH and extreme hypermobility increase the risk that individuals with no or moderate initial symptoms from a COVID-19 infection experience long COVIDLong COVID. Second, both GJH and extreme hypermobility are significant predictors of developing severe initial symptoms from a COVID-19 infection, which is independently associated with increased long COVID risk. A mediation analysis confirmed that extreme hypermobility influences the odds of developing long COVID in part by increasing the likelihood that individuals experience severe initial symptoms from a COVID infection.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: Both GJH and extreme hypermobility are significant risk factors for long COVID. People with extreme hypermobility, as newly defined in this study, are at particularly high risk of developing long COVID after an initial COVID-19 infection. Further research is needed to replicate these findings with other datasets, clarify the pathophysiology that explains why people with hypermobility may be at greater risk of long COVID and assess the clinical significance of ‘extreme hypermobility’.

notes.p: The lead authors affirm that the manuscript is an honest, accurate and transparent account of the study being reported; that no important aspects of the study have been omitted and that any discrepancies from the study as planned (and, if relevant, registered) have been explained.

sec.title: WHAT IS ALREADY KNOWN ON THIS TOPIC

list-item.p: Long COVID is a significant public health challenge.
Two prior studies have found an association between generalised joint hypermobility (GJH) and not recovering from COVID-19/long COVID.
No prior studies have examined whether and to what extent people who are particularly hypermobile are at risk of long COVID.
This study supports prior findings suggesting that the presence of GJH is a risk factor for not recovering fully from COVID-19.
While the 95% confidence intervals overlap, extreme hypermobility (as newly defined in this study) has a higher OR than GJH in predicting long COVID.

Exploratory analyses suggest that hypermobility may influence the odds of getting long COVID through two pathways: by increasing the incidence of severe initial infections and by increasing the likelihood that people with no or mild initial symptoms develop long COVID.

sec.title: HOW THIS STUDY MIGHT AFFECT RESEARCH, PRACTICE OR POLICY

list-item.p: Future research needs to characterise the nature of how variant connective tissue may contribute to the development of long COVID and other infection-associated chronic conditions.
Clinical studies need to develop and assess treatments that respond to people with long COVID who have co-occurring connective tissue disorders.
Future research should examine the clinical significance of ‘extreme hypermobility’ and develop a validated measure.

sec.title: Background
sec.p: Long COVID (also known as ‘post-acute sequelae of COVID-19’ (PASC), ‘post-acute COVID-19 syndrome’ and ‘post-COVID conditions’) is a condition characterised by long-lasting persistent or newly emerging symptoms following a COVID-19 infection (see information from the WHO1 and National Academies for Science2 for more background on the definition of this condition). This infection-associated chronic condition has emerged as a significant public health challenge. The most common among hundreds of documented symptoms are fatigue, postexertional symptom exacerbation/malaise, brain fog/cognitive dysfunction and shortness of breath.3 Symptoms may be new onset following initial recovery from an acute COVID-19 episode or persist from the initial illness without a gap, and they may fluctuate or exhibit a relapse/recovery pattern over time.4 Severity varies; some symptoms are mild, but the daily activities of around a quarter of people with long COVID are significantly limited by their symptoms,5 and many people with long COVID are completely bedbound. The burden of long COVID is considerable, affecting an estimated 400 million people globally and costing the global economy an estimated US$1 trillion every year.6 Understanding the factors that may predispose individuals to long COVID is crucial for preventing future cases, developing effective healthcare interventions and improving the quality of life for millions affected worldwide.

Connective tissue, which is ubiquitous throughout the body, plays a critical role in maintaining the structure and function of joints, organs, muscles, bones and nerves. Generalised joint hypermobility (GJH), which is found in approximately 20% of the UK population,7 is a sign of an underlying variation of connective tissue. While GJH is not always accompanied by problematic symptoms, certain clinical presentations of variant connective tissue, such as hypermobile Ehlers-Danlos Syndrome (hEDS; formerly known as EDS hypermobility type/EDS type-III) and hypermobility spectrum disorder (HSD), appear with significant clinical issues, including chronic fatigue and dysautonomia.8

Chronic health conditions such as fibromyalgia and myalgic encephalomyelitis/chronic fatigue syndrome (ME/CFS) frequently coexist with joint hypermobility and are often precipitated by viral infections, just as long COVID is precipitated by SARS-CoV-2. ME/CFS is characterised by three standard symptoms: (1) a substantial limitation in the ability to engage in life activities lasting at least 6 months that is accompanied by (generally profound) fatigue and not alleviated by rest, (2) postexertional malaise and (3) unrefreshing sleep, plus either (4a) cognitive impairment or (4b) orthostatic intolerance (or both).914 Dysautonomia and orthostatic intolerance are frequently experienced in both long COVID and GJH,4 for which there is a potential mechanistic link through the effect of connective tissue on venous return.15 A case-control study of 200 individuals with ME/CFS found that those with joint hypermobility experienced a greater reduction in cerebral blood flow from orthostatic stress than those without joint hypermobility, suggesting that joint hypermobility may contribute to orthostatic intolerance.16 Similarities and differences between ME/CFS and long COVID are subject to debate, but it is looking increasingly likely that a substantial subset of individuals with long COVID have a condition that is essentially similar to or the same as ME/CFS.17

Genetic variants affecting collagen and extracellular matrix proteins, a primary component of connective tissue, may alter how the body responds to viral infections and associated inflammation. Such alterations could potentially explain why individuals with connective tissue disorders often experience chronic health conditions following viral infections. Recent case-control evidence from both the British COVID Symptom Study Biobank (BCSSB) and the Mayo Clinic’s Long COVID Care Clinic has demonstrated a significant association between GJH and non-recovery from COVID-19.18 19 While the BCSSB study included a relatively large convenience sample (n=3064), both investigations were limited by participant samples that were predominantly white and female, which constrains the generalisability of their findings.

Given the relationships between variant connective tissue and postviral and other chronic conditions, it is imperative to explore the connection between variant connective tissue and long COVID in a broader sample than that described in the previous studies.18 19 Furthermore, it would be of interest to determine whether there were differences related to the degree of hypermobility. This is crucial to facilitate further research and clinical intervention for this often-underserved population.

sec.title: Objectives
sec.p: The primary objective of this study is to examine whether two measures of hypermobility—the established phenomenon of GJH, and our conceptualisation of the novel concept of ‘extreme hypermobility’—predict the development of long COVID. This research aims to enhance our understanding of how variant connective tissue influences the risk of long COVID.

sec.title: Setting
sec.p: This analysis is based on data collected through a survey conducted online via the platform Qualtrics, where participants accessed study information and provided online informed consent before accessing the survey. The study was approved by the Research Ethics and Governance Committee of the Brighton and Sussex Medical School (ER/BSMS9B02/6/4).

sec.title: Public and patient involvement
sec.p: The authorship team includes individuals with hypermobility and long COVID, as well as a patient caregiver. The survey was refined using the suggestions of additional patients with long COVID.

sec.title: Participants
sec.p: Before main recruitment, a pilot study was run on social media in December 2022 to test the feasibility of recruitment of respondents with and without long COVID. We assessed 833 participants for eligibility, 831 of whom were determined to be eligible for the study. However, most of these participants reported having long COVID (n=661), while recruitment of a sufficient number of respondents who did not report long COVID (n=170) was difficult. Therefore, we chose to engage the data service firm Dynata to support recruitment from representative samples for the main study and used data from the pilot study in sensitivity analyses only. The results reported here are based on the Dynata sample alone.

Participants were drawn from online panels that are representative of the general populations of the UK and USA through Dynata in January 2023. The recruitment aimed to identify at least 1800 eligible participants, which power calculations indicated would be sufficient to detect an OR of 1.19 in a logistic regression measuring the impact of GJH on long COVID in a two-tailed test at 80% power, assuming 20% of the sample reported long COVID and the observed frequencies of GJH in the pilot study. A total of 7119 potential participants were screened for eligibility criteria, which included age over 18 years, fluency in English and at least one COVID-19 infection contracted at least 3 months prior to taking the survey. A total of 1816 eligible respondents completed the survey and provided responses to the key items needed for the analysis; these respondents were included in the final study sample and divided into study groups (respondents reporting long COVID n=352, respondents not reporting long COVID n=1465). Responses from 58 eligible respondents were excluded based on poor data quality or a non-response to a critical element needed for the analysis. Several survey questions used sliders; for these questions, a non-response was interpreted as a zero.

sec.title: Long COVID
sec.p: Long COVID status was operationalised using participants’ responses to the question, ‘Have you been diagnosed with long COVID, or do you have symptoms that you attribute to long COVID? For the purposes of this survey, people have long COVID when they experience symptoms related to their COVID-19 infection at least 3 months after their initial infection’. Participants who selected the response, ‘Yes, a doctor has diagnosed me with long COVID’ or ‘Yes, I have symptoms I attribute to long COVID, but do not have a formal diagnosis’ were identified as reporting long COVID. Participants who selected the response ‘No, I do not have long COVID’ were identified as not reporting long COVID.

sec.title: Joint hypermobility
sec.p: Online supplemental figure 1 displays the questions and pictures used to assess joint hypermobility. GJH was assessed using the 5-part Hakim-Grahame self-report questionnaire (5PQ), with a score ≥2 indicating presence of GJH (online supplemental figure 1A).20 Extreme hypermobility was assessed using the 5PQ and the four additional questions shown in online supplemental figure 1B.

Since there is no widely accepted self-report measure of extreme hypermobility, the study team developed a novel measure based on the nine questions asked about hypermobility symptoms (online supplemental figure 1) and the core finding of a large study of 1000 individuals that the degree of hypermobility varies with age and gender.21 Based on this finding, the study team stratified the sample by age (18–39 years, 40–59 years, 60+ years) and sex assigned at birth into six groups. The study team ordered respondents within each group according to the number of the nine hypermobility questions they answered in the affirmative and then assigned percentiles within each group reflecting each individual’s place in the distribution within that group. Those in the top 10th percentile for hypermobility within their respective age and sex group were classified as having extreme hypermobility.

Because the study team’s methodology for defining extreme hypermobility is novel and not yet validated, it is important to include checks to ensure it does not lead to overfitting or other distortions. Accordingly, online supplemental table 4 presents results for an alternative definition of extreme hypermobility that does not stratify by age and gender as a sensitivity analysis.

sec.title: Statistical analysis
sec.p: Data analysis was performed using IBM SPSS Statistics V.29, MPlus and Jamovi V.2.3.28. Independent t-tests and χ2 tests were used to assess basic group differences for demographic and clinical variables. The data are unweighted.

A series of separate binomial logistic regressions was used to assess whether GJH and extreme hypermobility were predictive of self-reported long COVID. Each of these regressions controlled for age, sex at birth, number of infections (up to three) and number of vaccine doses, factors previously shown to affect the risk of long COVID.22

In an exploratory analysis, we used separate regression models for two sub-samples of respondents: (a) respondents who experienced no or mild symptoms during (all of) their COVID-19 infection(s) and (b) respondents who experienced severe symptoms (including but not limited to hospitalisation) during (any of) their COVID-19 infection(s).

Binomial logistic regressions were also used to examine whether GJH and extreme hypermobility were predictive of an individual experiencing severe symptoms (including but not limited to hospitalisation) during (any of) their COVID-19 infection(s). These regression models are the same as those used for the primary analyses with the exception that having severe symptoms during (any of) their COVID-19 infection(s) was used as the dependent variable rather than long COVID.

To further test whether having severe initial symptoms mediates the relationship between extreme hypermobility and long COVID, a mediation analysis was performed. MPlus V.823 was used to perform the mediation analysis with long COVID (presence/absence) as the binary outcome, extreme hypermobility as the binary predictor (presence/absence) and presence or absence of severe symptomatology during any COVID-19 infection(s) as the binary mediator. Probit regression was used with full information maximum likelihood (MPlus estimator command ML with Monte Carlo integration), applying bootstrapping (n=2000) with the generation of 95% bias-corrected bootstrapped CIs to inferentially assess direct and indirect mediation effects. Age, sex, number of infections and number of vaccinations were included as potential confounders.

To mitigate the risk that the specific procedures used to define extreme hypermobility impacted the findings, a sensitivity analysis was conducted using an alternative definition of extreme hypermobility. In this analysis, participants who answered ‘yes’ to six or more of the nine hypermobility questions were classified as having extreme hypermobility, which identifies a similar percentage of the sample as having extreme hypermobility as the main measure.
We also repeated all analyses with a sample that combined the Dynata and the pilot study participant samples, with an added control for sample source, as a further sensitivity analysis.

sec.title: Sample composition
sec.p: After data cleaning, the sample comprised 1816 respondents, 352 (19.4%) who reported long COVID and 1464 (80.6%) who did not. Demographic information is listed in table 1. Age and sex assigned at birth differed significantly between groups and were used as covariates in further analyses. Individuals in the long COVID group were more likely than those in the non-long COVID group to report a parent, child or sibling with long COVID, suggesting the possibility of a familial predisposition to the condition.

tr.td: % reporting a parent, child or sibling with long COVID

fn.p: Note: values are n, % unless otherwise specified.

sec.title: COVID-19 infections
sec.p: Summary data regarding the number and severity of COVID-19 infections and number of vaccines are listed in table 2. In line with previous findings,24 the long COVID group was more likely to report two or more COVID-19 infections than the group not reporting long COVID. We found a significant difference in symptom severity where long COVID respondents were more likely to report experiencing severe initial symptoms during one or more of their COVID-19 infections.

caption.title: Long COVID group and non-long COVID group frequencies in categories of infections, vaccines, initial severity, recovery and hypermobility

tr.td: Mild or no initial symptoms from (all) COVID-19 infection(s)
Severe initial symptoms from any COVID-19 infection
Admitted to hospital but not ICU
No or small amount of recovery

fn.p: Note: individual components may not add up to the total due to rounding.
GJH, generalised joint hypermobility; ICU, intensive care unit.

sec.p: As would be expected, people reporting long COVID more often reported they had experienced no recovery, a small recovery or only a moderate recovery from their COVID-19 infection(s). However, about a third of those reporting having experienced long COVID said they had fully recovered, and another 22% had experienced a substantial recovery by the time they completed the survey questionnaire.

sec.title: Joint hypermobility
sec.p: We found a significant group difference in the presence of extreme hypermobility, but not in the presence of GJH (table 2).

sec.title: Relationship between joint hypermobility and long COVID
sec.p: Table 3 summarises the results of separate serial binomial logistic regressions that assess the extent to which GJH (online supplemental table 1) and extreme hypermobility (online supplemental table 2) are predictors of long COVID.

caption.title: Binomial logistic regressions with long COVID as the dependent variable

tr.th: Mild or no initial symptoms during all infections
Severe initial symptoms during any infection

fn.p: Note: this table reports the results of six separate binominal logistic regressions that each control for: sex at birth, age, number of infections (up to three) and number of vaccine doses. The regressions in the first row include generalised joint hypermobility (GJH) as an independent variable. The regressions in the second row include extreme hypermobility as an independent variable. The "Full sample" column shows the regression results for the analyses conducted to answer the primary research questions of whether GJH and extreme hypermobility are predictive of long COVID.

sec.p: Controlling for sex at birth, age, number of infections (up to three) and number of vaccine doses, GJH was found to be predictive of long COVID, with an OR of 1.29 (95% CI 1.00 to 1.65), and extreme hypermobility was found to be predictive of long COVID with an OR of 2.12 (95% CI 1.43 to 3.16). Age, number of infections (up to three) and sex at birth were also significant predictors of long COVID in both of these regressions.

The next set of binomial logistic regressions in table 3 shows the results for individuals with mild or no initial symptoms during all infections. In this subgroup, both GJH (OR 1.54, 95% CI 1.05 to 2.25) and extreme hypermobility (OR 2.59, 95% CI 1.37 to 4.89) are significantly associated with long COVID. Neither hypermobility measure is a significant predictor of long COVID among the subgroup of respondents with severe initial symptoms during any COVID-19 infection.

Figure 1 illustrates an exploratory analysis of the incidence of long COVID in the combined sample by decile of hypermobility based on the age-adjusted and gender-adjusted measure for those with mild or no initial symptoms from (all) infection(s) (blue line) and those with severe initial symptoms from any infection (orange line).

caption.title: Incidence of long COVID by (age-adjusted and sex-adjusted) decile of hypermobility in participants with mild or no initial symptoms (orange) and severe initial symptoms (blue) after COVID-19 infection.

sec.p: The exploratory analysis suggests that the incidence of long COVID may increase as respondents’ hypermobility percentile increases, with a notable spike between the 90th and 100th percentiles, suggesting a ceiling effect of participants with the most extreme hypermobility. This study was not sized to detect significant differences between deciles of hypermobility; a larger study would be required to assess this issue more definitively. However, the regression findings documented here confirm that being in the top 10th percentile of hypermobility is a significant predictor of long COVID for the full sample and people with mild or no initial symptoms from a COVID-19 infection.

sec.title: Inter-relationship of hypermobility and severity of initial COVID infection(s)
sec.p: GJH and extreme hypermobility appear to increase the risk of long COVID through two mechanisms. First, as demonstrated here (table 3), both GJH and extreme hypermobility are associated with an increased risk that people with mild or no initial symptoms from COVID-19 will develop long COVID. Second, both GJH (OR 1.46, 95% CI 1.18 to 1.80) and extreme hypermobility (OR 2.21, 95% CI 1.53 to 3.20) are significant predictors of severe initial symptoms during one or more infection(s) in regressions controlling for sex at birth, age, number of infections (up to three) and number of vaccine doses. Having had severe initial symptoms from any COVID-19 infection is an independent predictor of long COVID, with an O.R. of 3.60 (95% CI 2.79 to 4.66) in a regression controlling for sex at birth, age, number of infections (up to three), number of vaccine doses and extreme hypermobility.

To further test whether having severe initial symptoms mediates the relationship between extreme hypermobility and long COVID, a mediation analysis was performed. As illustrated in figure 2, the analysis shows that extreme hypermobility is both directly associated with long COVID and indirectly associated with long COVID through the mediating variable of severe initial symptoms during at least one COVID-19 infection.

caption.title: Mediation model, demonstrating mediating link of severe symptoms in the relation between extreme hypermobility and long COVID, adjusted for potential confounders. Note: *p<0.05, **p<0.01, ***p<0.001.

sec.p: In the mediation analysis, we found a significant relationship between number of infections and presence of long COVID, but other covariates, including age, sex and number of vaccine doses did not have an impact on the development of long COVID.

sec.title: Sensitivity analyses
sec.p: As a sensitivity analysis, the association between extreme hypermobility and long COVID was assessed through an alternative approach that assigned everyone who answered 'Yes' to six or more of the nine hypermobility questions to the category of extreme hypermobility, without sorting by age or gender. Roughly 7.4% of the combined sample meets this alternative definition of extreme hypermobility, which is very similar to the 7.3% of respondents with extreme hypermobility under this study’s primary measure. In a regression controlling for sex at birth, age, number of infections (up to three) and number of vaccine doses, extreme hypermobility under this alternative definition was significantly associated with long COVID with an OR of 1.74 (95% CI 1.14 to 2.65), compared with the OR of 2.12 using our primary measure of extreme hypermobility.

Online supplemental table 4 shows the regression results for this alternative definition of extreme hypermobility side by side with the results using the primary definition. As the table illustrates, the results for both definitions are essentially similar for the full sample and for the sample of respondents with severe initial symptoms from one or more COVID-19 infections. However, for the sample of respondents with mild or no initial symptoms from a COVID-19 infection, the results for the primary definition are statistically significant (OR 2.59, 95% CI 1.37 to 4.89, p=0.003) while those of the alternative definition are not (OR 1.77, 95% CI 0.86 to 3.65, p=0.122).

To test the effects of omitting the pilot data collected through recruitment via social media, the binomial logistic regressions reported in this study were also performed on a dataset that combined the social media sample with the Dynata sample used in this study, with an added control for dataset. These regressions produced results that were substantially similar to those reported here. A meta-analysis is underway to analyse the association between hypermobility and long COVID in these two samples and other relevant datasets.

sec.title: Association between hypermobility and long COVID
sec.p: Our results both confirm and extend prior research. Tracking case-control evidence from the BCSSB18 and a study of 732 patients with long COVID at Mayo Clinics in two US cities,19 this study finds GJH significantly associated with long COVID. This study extends past research by examining the risk of long COVID associated with having extreme hypermobility. While the 95% CIs overlap, extreme hypermobility has a higher OR than GJH in predicting long COVID. Further research is needed to examine whether this difference would be statistically significant with a larger sample.

This study used a novel measure of extreme hypermobility developed specifically for this analysis. To reduce the risk that the study team’s definition of this novel concept affected the findings, a sensitivity analysis was conducted using an alternative definition of extreme hypermobility. This sensitivity analysis found that the alternative definition was predictive of long COVID in the full sample but not in the sample of individuals with mild or no initial symptoms from (a) COVID-19 infection(s); this suggests the alternative definition is somewhat less effective than the primary definition in predicting long COVID. Further research is needed to develop a fully validated measure of extreme hypermobility that is reproducible and conforms to clinical observations—and to better understand the extent to which the concept of extreme hypermobility has other implications for clinical practice.

The results suggest that hypermobility affects the risk of long COVID through two mechanisms. First, both measures of hypermobility are significantly associated with an increased risk of long COVID among respondents with no or mild initial symptoms from (all) COVID-19 infection(s). Second, both measures of hypermobility are significantly associated with the likelihood of respondents experiencing severe initial symptoms from a COVID-19 infection, which is independently and strongly associated with long COVID.

Neither of the hypermobility variables is significantly associated with respondent-reported long COVID among the subgroup of respondents that experienced severe initial symptoms from one or more COVID-19 infections. To the extent that severe initial symptoms reflect damage from a serious inflammatory cascade or other severe manifestation of COVID-19, it makes sense that people without a connective tissue disorder may be just as likely as people with a connective tissue disorder to experience long-lasting effects. The relationship between hypermobility and long COVID among those experiencing severe initial symptoms from the infection might change over time if and to the extent people with hypermobility are less likely to recover or recover more slowly from damage caused by the initial inflammatory cascade or other severe initial effect of a COVID-19 infection.

sec.title: Relationship between connective tissue disorders and long COVID
sec.p: Further research is needed to replicate these analyses with other contexts and methods. The optimal study would have measured GJH and extreme hypermobility prospectively, before individuals are exposed to COVID-19. Since most people have been exposed to COVID-19 at least once at this point, this may be impossible to implement for COVID-19. With other pathogens on the horizon, however, prospective studies of other infection-associated chronic conditions may be possible if GJH and extreme hypermobility become more commonly measured. It will also be important to assess the risk of long COVID and other infection-associated chronic conditions stemming from other connective tissue disorders, such as Sjögren’s syndrome, systemic lupus erythematosus and rheumatoid arthritis. A study of 15 676 patients in the JH-Crown Consortium database found that 9.52% of patients with one of three connective tissue disorders developed PASC as opposed to 2.54% of the entire cohort.25 While helpfully considering a broader array of connective tissue orders than just hEDS, the study noted that the incidence of connective tissue disorders was likely underestimated due to lack of awareness of connective tissue disorders outside of specialised clinics; this highlights the importance of merging hospital records with survey data assessing hypermobility.

Research is also needed to examine the extent to which individuals experience greater hypermobility after an infection and whether impacts on the extent of hypermobility are limited to or concentrated on individuals with an underlying connective tissue disorder or experienced by others. As reflected in online supplemental table 3, the responses of the median respondent with extreme hypermobility suggested that their current hypermobility was more or less unchanged relative to the level before their infection. However, significant shares of respondents with extreme hypermobility reported that they had grown more or less hypermobile since getting COVID-19.

Finally, research is needed to better understand why people with hypermobility may be at greater risk of developing long COVID and what this suggests about the pathophysiology of this chronic condition. There are at least six possible explanations for why people with hypermobility may be at higher risk of long COVID that have been suggested in or can be derived from the literature; further research is needed to determine which, if any, are valid.

First, some have suggested that vascular laxity makes people with EDS vulnerable to orthostatic intolerance due to the distension of veins from hydrostatic pressure, which increases venous pooling,13 14 26 which is characteristic of a ME/CFS-type presentation of long COVID. The finding that, among people with ME/CFS, those with joint hypermobility experience a greater reduction in cerebral blood flow from orthostatic stress than those without joint hypermobility is consistent with this hypothesis.16

A second explanation is that people with EDS or hypermobility spectrum disorder are more susceptible to vascular compression syndromes such as May-Thurner syndrome. A study of 45 women with venous origin chronic pelvic pain found substantial improvement from surgical treatment in a broad range of symptoms overlapping with ME/CFS and long COVID; 56% of these patients had EDS or hypermobility spectrum disorder.27

A third explanation is that the inter-relationship of connective tissue and mast cells leads people with EDS or hypermobility spectrum disorder to be particularly vulnerable to mast cell activation disorders,28 which can in turn prompt both postural orthostatic tachycardia syndrome (POTS) and conditions like long COVID and ME/CFS.29 30

A fourth possibility is that the increased risk of long COVID is related to the susceptibility of people with EDS to autoimmune disorders31; an increasing body of evidence documents the role that autoantibodies play in long COVID.32 33

A fifth possibility is that people with connective tissue disorders are more likely to experience and be slower to recover from the vascular damage that characterises long COVID and associated damage to the extracellular matrix.34 35

Finally, the increased long COVID risk from hypermobility could be related to the tendency of people with EDS to experience spinal problems such as Chiari malformation and craniocervical instability.10 36 37 As vascular and blood-brain barrier permeability increases among people with long COVID due to inflammation and vascular damage,38 39 the resulting leakage of protein-rich fluid in the brain can increase the volume of glymphatic and cerebrospinal fluid in need of drainage. Spinal problems such as Chiari malformation and craniocervical instability can impair the drainage of cerebrospinal fluid, resulting in an increase in intracranial pressure that leads to brainstem compression;40 an impaired glymphatic system may compound the problem.34 41 Drawing on the experience of patients in their practice, Maxwell and Wardly have proposed an overarching hypothesis that ties together several of these mechanisms, including mast cell activation, hypermobility, dysautonomia and spinal complications.42

These and other possibilities should be rigorously assessed to determine whether they are valid explanations for the increased risk of long COVID associated with hypermobility. This research may shed important light on the field’s understanding of the pathophysiology of long COVID, which remains incomplete.

sec.title: Strengths and limitations
sec.p: This study has several strengths. First, recruitment from representative online panels means that this study’s results are more generalisable to the adult population than studies with convenience samples. Second, employing a validated diagnostic questionnaire for hypermobility (the 5PQ) ensures that this study is comparable with others that use this measure. Third, the additional questions used to operationalise extreme hypermobility have the advantage of addressing hypermobility in body parts (feet and skin) and orientations (W-sitting) not considered in the 5PQ. The concept of extreme hypermobility has not been previously studied and represents an important innovation of this study; we found our proposed measure of extreme hypermobility to be a better predictor of long COVID morbidity than the more standard GJH.

A key limitation of this study is the reliance on self-reported data collected at a single point in time, rather than longitudinally. Respondents may or may not have a good recollection of their condition before getting COVID-19. In addition, the measure of extreme hypermobility is novel and not yet validated, and both the measure of GJH and the measure of extreme hypermobility rely on patient responses to survey questions rather than direct clinical observation, introducing the possibility that individuals are misclassified or that the classification is affected by subjective or cultural factors. Another limitation is that the study does not include comorbid conditions in the regression analysis. The survey collected data on 25 categories of pre-existing conditions, all of which were reported by a greater share of people with extreme hypermobility than by other study respondents. Understanding and untangling the relationship of each of these conditions to hypermobility and the extent to which each might moderate or otherwise influence the relationship between hypermobility and long COVID is an important but complicated inquiry that we plan to address in a future paper. Finally, socioeconomic factors are not included in the regression analyses; race and ethnicity data were collected but not included in the analyses because they did not differ significantly between the groups reporting and not reporting long COVID.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: These findings suggest that people with variant connective tissue (manifest as joint hypermobility) disproportionately experience long COVID. Long COVID among people with variant connective tissue may potentially constitute a subtype of long COVID, a heterogenous condition. Better recognition and enhanced understanding of this association is necessary to address the public health challenges of long COVID and enhance personalised care of often underserved groups.43

ack.title: Acknowledgements
ack.p: We are grateful to Eric Hedberg for valuable statistical advice and review of the manuscript and to the individuals with long COVID who provided feedback on the initial questionnaire and shared the pilot questionnaire via Social Media.

fn.p: Funding: This study was partially funded by the Ehlers-Danlos Syndrome Research Foundation (no award number given) and The Ehlers-Danlos Society (no award number given). However, the funders played no role in the research, which was conducted independently by the authors.
Provenance and peer review: Not commissioned; externally peer reviewed.
Patient consent for publication: Not applicable.

Ethics approval: This study involves human participants and the study was approved by the Research Ethics and Governance Committee of the Brighton and Sussex Medical School (ER/BSMS9B02/6/4). Participants gave informed consent to participate in the study before taking part.
Patient and public involvement: Patients and/or the public were involved in the design, conduct, reporting or dissemination plans of this research. Refer to the Methods section for further details.

sec.title: Data availability statement
sec.p: Data are available upon reasonable request.
